none: DRAFT: TARP Calumet tunnel system : environmental impact statement.

The Calumet Tunnel System is part of the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago. The project aims to reduce flooding and improve water quality in the Chicago area by constructing a series of tunnels and reservoirs. The tunnels will capture and treat stormwater and sewage overflow, which currently pollutes local waterways. This initiative is crucial for protecting Lake Michigan and enhancing the quality of life for residents by reducing pollution and flood risks.
DRAFT: TARP Calumet tunnel system : environmental impact statement.

The Calumet Tunnel System is part of the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago. The project aims to reduce flooding and improve water quality in the Chicago area by constructing a series of tunnels and reservoirs. The tunnels will capture and treat stormwater and sewage overflow, which currently pollutes local waterways. This initiative is crucial for protecting Lake Michigan and enhancing the quality of life for residents by reducing pollution and flood risks.
Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago

The Calumet Tunnel System was proposed as part of a broader effort to address chronic flooding and water quality issues in the Chicago area. There was some public concern regarding the environmental impacts and costs associated with the project, but it was ultimately seen as necessary for protecting local waterways and Lake Michigan. The project has been a significant undertaking aimed at improving urban infrastructure and environmental health.

"The tunnels will capture approximately 90 percent of the pollutant load now discharged during combined-sewer overflows and will reduce the pollutant load 75 percent overall." — Principal Findings Concerning the Effects of the Proposed Action

"The flooding problem existing in the Chicago area will not be resolved by the Phase I tunnels. Overflow to Lake Michigan will still persist if the proposed reservoirs are not implemented." — Effects of Flooding on Lake Michigan
